subject: AP US Government and Politics
theme: Political participation in the United States is shaped by structural factors, voting rights, party competition, interest group activity, campaign finance, and media — together these linkage institutions connect citizens to a government that depends on their engagement for democratic legitimacy.
core_concepts: Voting rights have been progressively expanded through constitutional amendments: 15th (race, 1870), 19th (sex, 1920), 24th (poll taxes, 1964), 26th (age 18, 1971) — and through legislation including the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

Voter turnout is persistently lower in the U.S. than in most democracies — registration requirements, election timing, and structural barriers reduce participation, while education, income, and age increase it.

Voting behavior is shaped by party identification (strongest predictor), retrospective evaluation (past performance), prospective voting (future promises), and demographic factors including race, gender, religion, and education.

The two-party system persists due to winner-take-all (single-member plurality) electoral rules (Duverger's Law), ballot access barriers, and campaign finance advantages — third parties influence elections as spoilers and policy innovators but rarely win.

Political parties serve as linkage institutions: they nominate candidates, mobilize voters, develop platforms, and organize government — dealignment (declining party identification) and polarization (more ideologically distinct parties) are competing contemporary trends.

Interest groups influence policy through direct lobbying, grassroots lobbying, PAC/Super PAC spending, litigation, and coalition building — the iron triangle (agency + congressional subcommittee + interest group) describes captured policy relationships.

Citizens United v. FEC (2010) held that independent expenditures by corporations and unions are protected First Amendment speech — enabling Super PACs to raise and spend unlimited money on elections, fundamentally transforming campaign finance.

Media serves as a linkage institution and democratic 'fourth estate' — agenda setting, framing, and priming shape how citizens understand politics; horse-race coverage, partisan media, and social media echo chambers challenge the informed citizenry that democracy requires.

key_terms.definition: The legal right to vote — expanded progressively through constitutional amendments and legislation from propertied white men to all citizens 18+.
The percentage of eligible voters who cast ballots in an election — higher in presidential years; influenced by registration rules, election salience, and demographic factors.
Choosing candidates based on past performance — rewarding incumbents when times are good, punishing them when times are bad.
Choosing candidates based on their future policy promises and platform positions.
A stable psychological attachment to a political party formed through socialization — the strongest predictor of voting behavior in U.S. elections.
Voting for candidates from different parties in the same election — has declined as partisan polarization has increased.
An election to choose party nominees in which only registered members of that party may vote.
An election to choose party nominees in which any registered voter may participate regardless of their party registration.
The constitutional body of 538 electors who formally elect the president — a candidate needs 270 electoral votes to win; most states use a winner-take-all system.
A durable, lasting shift in the partisan coalitions — when major voter groups permanently shift party allegiance, creating a new majority coalition (e.g., New Deal coalition of 1932).
A weakening of party loyalty without a corresponding shift to the other party — rising numbers of independents who feel unrepresented by either major party.
The political science principle that single-member plurality electoral systems tend to produce and sustain two-party systems by punishing votes for third-party candidates.
Organizations connecting citizens to government — includes political parties, interest groups, elections, and the media.

The mutually reinforcing relationship among a congressional subcommittee, an executive agency, and an interest group — each provides benefits to the others in a stable, self-reinforcing policy subsystem.
An independent expenditure-only committee that can accept unlimited contributions from corporations, unions, and individuals — may not coordinate with candidates or contribute directly to campaigns.
The tendency for individuals to benefit from a group's collective advocacy without joining or contributing — a barrier to interest group formation and maintenance.
Regulated campaign contributions subject to FECA limits, made directly to candidates or parties for federal elections.
Unlimited contributions to parties for 'party-building' activities — banned by BCRA (2002) for national parties but channeled through Super PACs and 501(c)(4) organizations.
The media's power to determine which issues the public considers important by choosing which stories to cover prominently.
The way media presents issues shapes how audiences interpret causes, consequences, and solutions — the same event can be framed differently with different political implications.
The media's ability to influence which criteria citizens use to evaluate political leaders — heavy coverage of the economy makes voters evaluate leaders on economic performance.
The structural benefits enjoyed by sitting officeholders — name recognition, franking privilege, casework, established donor networks, and (for Congress) potential gerrymandering of their district.

exam_tip: Unit 5 questions often pair a data stimulus (table or chart showing voter turnout by demographic, party identification trends, or campaign spending data) with a question asking you to draw a supported conclusion — practice reading trends carefully and avoiding overgeneralizations not supported by the specific data shown.
